heading: TONUS QUARTUS — Quid sit veritas. Caput I
card_summary: This chapter opens Tonus Quartus with a Kabbalistic-philosophical inquiry into the nature of truth (veritas). Zorzi argues that truth is too brilliant for weak minds, perceptible only by the intellect, and that its essence lies in the concordance of middle with beginning and end — a definition drawn from Anselm of Canterbury's formula "rectitudo sola mente perceptibilis." The chapter's centerpiece is a detailed Hebrew analysis of the word emet (aleph-mem-tau), showing how its three letters — first, middle, and last of the alphabet — encode the structure of all reality as procession from and return to the divine principle. Zorzi further contrasts emet with sheqer (shin-qof-resh), the Hebrew word for falsehood, whose letters lack stable bases and cannot stand independently, embodying the proverbial "mendacium breves pedes habet." The Italian editorial heading identifies Christ as the supreme truth and light.
essay:
## The Blinding Splendor of Truth

Zorzi opens *Tonus Quartus* with a striking epistemological claim: truth is so radiant that it overwhelms weak eyes and exceeds the capacity of those of limited intellectual virtue. Common souls (*vulgarium animi*), not yet purified, take more pleasure in coarse falsehood than in subtle truth. This sets the tone for a chapter that moves from epistemology into Kabbalistic linguistics.

> Tantus est veritatis splendor, ut omnes debiles oculos opprimat, et parvam potentiae virtutem excedens offendat, hinc vulgarium animi, et non adhuc bene expiati magis crasso mendacio, quam subtili veritate oblectantur.

<cite>p. 1550</cite>

The definition of truth Zorzi invokes — *rectitudo sola mente perceptibilis* — is the classical Anselmian formula from *De Veritate*, here attributed to "sapientes" by common consent. Truth is perceivable only by the intellect (*mens*), and thus unavailable to those who do not possess mind in the full philosophical sense — those who do not "sapiunt ea quae Dei sunt," who have no taste for divine things. God himself is identified as *fons totius veritatis*, and truth's defining property is *concordia et convenientia medii cum principio et fine* — the agreement of the middle with the beginning and end.

## The Kabbalistic Analysis of Emet (אמת)

This ontological definition of truth as the harmony of beginning, middle, and end is then confirmed — Zorzi says, "not without great mystery" (*mysterio non modico*) — by the structure of the Hebrew word for truth, *emet* (אמת), in "the most ancient language, which is called holy" (*antiquissima lingua quae sancta dicitur*).

> Hinc mysterio non modico in antiquissima lingua (quae sancta dicitur) veritas sic describitur אמת, in quo verbo prima litera alphabeti, media, et extrema connexae conveniunt. Nam א aleph est prima, ת tau ultima, מ mem vero media, et cum earum punctaturis profertur emet.

<cite>p. 1550</cite>

The three letters of *emet* are:
- **Aleph** (א) — the first letter of the Hebrew alphabet
- **Mem** (מ) — the middle letter
- **Tau** (ת) — the final letter

Their combination, Zorzi explains, indicates "the entire fabric of things" (*tota rerum textura*): the going-out from the principle into reality itself, and the return to the end. This is a classic Neoplatonic triad — *exitus*, *conversio*, *reditus* — now found encoded in the very orthography of the Hebrew language, confirming for Zorzi that sacred Hebrew is the language of divine structure itself.

## The Stability of Truth: Square Letters and Quaternary Numbers

Zorzi extends the analysis into graphological and numerological dimensions. The name *emet* is composed of letters that are "doubly square" (*bifarie quadratis*): both in their visual form (each letter has firm feet and bases on which it can stand independently and collectively) and in their numerical values.

> Ad denotandam firmitudinem, stabilitatemque veritatis, eius nomen componitur ex literis bifarie quadratis, figura videlicet et numeris. Figura enim ipsarum literarum ea est, quae firmos habeat pedes, et bases, quibus stet quaelibet earum seorsum, et fortius omne simul.

<cite>pp. 1550–1551</cite>

The numerical dimension: each letter of *emet* carries the quaternary number (4), which is *solidum et firmum* — the solid, stable number. Aleph signifies both 1 and 1000: the one as the firm principle and essence of all numbers, and 1000 as completion and fullness.

## Falsehood as Instability: Sheqer (שקר)

In deliberate contrast, Zorzi introduces the Hebrew word for falsehood, *sheqer* (שקר), whose letters — shin, qof, resh — have no stable bases and cannot stand independently or together. This visual and numerical instability of falsehood confirms the ancient adage:

> Mendacium breves pedes habet, immo claudos, et debiles. Sic enim illud nomen scribitur שקר; quod proferentes nostra lingua dicimus secher.

<cite>p. 1551</cite>

The contrast between *emet* and *sheqer* is thus architectural: truth stands firm on broad square foundations; falsehood limps on lame and weak feet. The Hebrew alphabet itself, for Zorzi, is a divinely constructed system in which theological truths are embedded in the shapes and values of the letters — a cornerstone assumption of Christian Kabbalism as developed by Pico della Mirandola, Johannes Reuchlin, and Zorzi himself.

## The Italian Heading: Christ as Supreme Truth

The Italian editorial section heading supplied in this chunk — "Cristo è la suprema verità e la luce" ("Christ is the supreme truth and the light") — indicates that this chapter is ultimately christological. The abstract Kabbalistic linguistics and Neoplatonic epistemology are pointing toward an identification of Christ with the cosmic principle of truth encoded in *emet*: the Alpha and Omega (= Aleph and Tau) with the mediating principle (Mem) between them. This connects to the wider Johannine theology of Christ as *Veritas* and *Lux mundi* (John 14:6, 8:12), and to the Kabbalistic identification of *emet* as one of the divine attributes (*middot*).

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chapter is structurally pivotal: it inaugurates *Tonus Quartus* with a meditation on truth that synthesizes Anselmian epistemology, Neoplatonic procession-and-return cosmology, and Kabbalistic letter-mysticism into a single unified argument. The identification of the triad aleph-mem-tau with the cosmic structure of beginning-middle-end mirrors Zorzi's broader harmonic cosmology, in which all levels of reality — numerical, musical, linguistic, theological — reflect the same triadic structure emanating from the divine one and returning to it. The contrast of *emet*/*sheqer* also participates in Zorzi's recurring theme of the conflict between divine wisdom (accessible only to the purified intellect) and popular delusion.
